Okay, we are picking up where we left off, which is a page that looks like this. You will recall we are in Toolbox, and we are in the drawer of the product slash message. And three things to keep in mind about that are these. One is you definitely want a thing, a system, a plan, a company, a certification, and identifying mark, a badge they can wear. You want a thing from which they can draw confidence and on which they place value. And sort of the messiness in the marketplace conversation. See if you took the ability to say Jeff Walker trained product launch person out of that, all the value is really still there, but not to the buyer. Because that's the thing he or she are really hanging their whole hat on. It's what they are drawing their confidence from. So you want a thing. You obviously, we've talked about it, you need a person that they are attached to, and you need a sense of place. So nobody really is eager to buy improvement or opportunity as a one and done. Now logically, so again we got to be careful that it's not us. So here's how I would prefer, and in many respects you may be the same. So here's how I would prefer to say add a million dollars to my income next year, like in a few days without working. I would prefer not to have to come and sit in a seminar to learn how to do it. I would prefer not to have to have any kind of ongoing relationship with anybody. If you could just write it on a four by six card and give it to me, I'm happy to write you a big check for it. That's me. Now really you would think, well should everybody ought to be happy with that? It's the last thing they want. And not only would of course they be suspicious of it to the point that it would be difficult to sell it, but beyond that, that's not what people want. They want to plug into something, and they kind of want to stay plugged. They also leave after a time, but that's another story. What they want when they buy is they want to plug into something. So like in Bill Driscoll's business, if he said look, here we'll put you to fire alarm business, and by the way here's these molds you can use and you can make the fire alarms in your kitchen out of trash, out of the trash basket, so you don't have to buy any alarms from us. And so we'll just go teach it to you, and here you're in the business. That'd be harder to sell than what he's selling now. So you can keep all the money. You don't have to buy anything from us. If you give us any money, we'll teach you the business, turn you loose. That's not what they want. And so this third thing is kind of important. You have to have something they're plugging into and staying plugged into when you present opportunity to people. I'm going to skip a couple of things for the sake of time. What do I want to do? What do I want to do? Well, let me just show you a couple of examples, I guess, real quick before we go on. So some number of pages over from where we were, you'll find a page like this, Opportunity is Reinvention. So just show you a couple of examples before we move on to the next big topic. So we talked about how cash practice was a reinvention. I showed you that as an example. Selling reinvention against the enemy. So we talked about enemy on the list for salespeople at its home office. So now if you put these two things together, where the reinvention is also the answer to the thing that is making them miserable to the villain. So the latest version of that for a client of mine in chiropractic, we've made the villain all the marketing systems everybody's selling. You might think of that as biting the hand, but nonetheless. So literally this is actually about the last thing you need now is another marketing system. And instead you need to completely reinvent the practice and here's how we're going to do that. In this ad, we've made the enemy my friend. And so your headline is can you re-engineer your practice to be safe from Obamacare or other nationalized health care or other government interference, intense competition, all the other evils. And no, not by making superficial incremental changes, back to the last thing you need, is another marketing system. I've also given you a couple of replacement examples that follow. And you can look at those on your own time. But there's a couple of examples now of replacement copy. So now let's talk about the thing itself. So when you go to structure this opportunity thing now, if you're going to fool with the thing itself and sort of not just how you talk about it, just keep turning them over. You'll get there eventually. I promise you it's there. So really the two issues, the two issues are structure and how you get paid. Those are the two issues that you confront, right? But how do we get a structure of this thing we sell and how are we going to get paid for? And so the structures are basically these things or combinations thereof, right? We could actually have a franchise. And we do have info marketers in niches who make the shift, so you're considering it or close to it or whatever. We do have info marketers. You're in the franchise business, although not an info marketer, with UPS stores. Probably on Planet Dan at the moment, Tom Shack is the best known with health source, who I think is up to 360, 370, 380 clinics in chiropractic in now maybe what, four, four and a half years. From four. And by the way, nobody ever made it work in chiropractic and many times it's been tried before, but this puppy works. So there is actually a real franchise, you know, like you hire a real lawyer and there's real documents and there's real registration and there's real fees and there's real royalties and there's enormous control. So there's a franchise. And there's like almost franchises, which are structured in such a way as to not have to register them, not have to take as much control and absorb as much responsibility, but they're close. They flirt. And essentially in a direct sales industry where Bill lives, a dealership is close, but not quite. And most of the air exclusive programs that info marketers do are close, but not quite. And so in both of those cases, the sort of the big piece of leverage you always have is some version of limitation and exclusivity. That's the big piece of leverage that you have because people want it more if only one can have it. I mean, and if there's a risk of somebody getting it that they don't like, that's even better. Then you have distributorship if they're going to sell stuff of yours. You have a consultant opportunity with or without certification language, as Jeff just described. You have sort of the more traditional coaching plus where people are being paying to be in a group or not in a group, but getting on the phone. So again, a coaching relationship plus they're getting a bunch of stuff they can use and maybe exclusive access to stuff nobody else can use. Vendors behind a wall who will do certain things for them. So like in the client I'm working with in financial services, so we have like Dean Killingbeck and birthday mailings. So as a vendor to them, which, but we have exclusive birthday mailings that only the guys in this program can use. So the content is exclusive. The vendor really isn't, but the combination of the two are exclusive. You have benefits and services plus coaching where all the benefits is on the, all the emphasis is on the stuff, what is being done for them. And the coaching is sort of the oh by the way, you have just outright services and you have one time events training or one time event training plus a bunch of stuff attached to it. So those are your structural options of any significance. And then how you get paid, you can get paid by fees. You can get paid by fees plus profits on services used, which you may or may not choose to disclose by the way. In some relationships it's all fully disclosed, in other relationships it's hidden. You can have fees plus a share in the upside, which is one of the questions it was just to ask, right? And like everything else, it has pluses and minuses and this seminar is not about that, so this is really a quick review of these things. But percentage of upsides tend to be, of course if you're in a real franchise relationship you're sharing in the gross, not just the upside. So sharing an upside tends to be some version of what's called in practice management percentage above base, where some base of where they are is established and then you're sharing in the increase or like in Lisa Miller's business she's sharing in the cost savings by percentage. There's fees plus some share in the upside plus getting profits on the services that they're buying. So you get in money three places. There's some situation structured where it's all upside, no fee, upside plus service and some people take equity. So there are businesses where literally, and it's actually alternative to franchise if you eat at a like a Hyde Park Grill, those are 50% corporate owned and 50% owned by an individual partner in that unit by unit by unit by unit. So it's a different approach to franchising, it's actually partnership and there's a share in the equity. So those are like your structure options. The exclusivity issue if you're not fully familiar with it, it dramatically helps the sale of an opportunity if it includes some type, some type of exclusivity for all these reasons. For all these reasons. So one of the big things people worry about, right, when they buy improvement or opportunity is well how's it going to help me if a whole bunch of guys in my business in my area are using the same stuff. And so that's in their head. As soon as they start to accept the idea that what you've got is good and will be useful to them, the next thing they start worrying about is who else in my area has it? And who else in my area is going to have the right to use it too? That's the very next thing they start sweating bullets over. They haven't really got it yet, they haven't started using it, they haven't used anything they've ever bought in their entire life, but they're worried now that there may be four other people who have the same stuff. And if you don't think it's an obstacle to their buying, you're wrong. It's at every level, low dollar amount, high dollar amount, doesn't make any difference, right? Well no, there won't be anybody else in each two below using the stuff, there's only going to be one. The only question is, you or somebody else? I wouldn't recommend it, but the way like Jeff could have done it is either area or he could have done it by type of business. So you get to be the product launch person for everybody that is in the get rich in real estate world and you get to be the product launch person for everybody who's in the get rich income quads world and you get to be in nutrition and now you'd have to charge a whole lot more money, right? But it is an answer. Second reason it works is because they start to focus on who they don't want to have this thing, right? And that's a very powerful motivator, particularly in local geographic markets and enclosed niches. So when you're selling to dentists, chiropractors, real estate agents, insurance agents, hair salon owners, gym owners, whatever, you know that there is somebody in their market area in their business they hate. It's usually, not always, but it's usually the most successful one who also tends to be the most visible. So every time Joe the lawyer clicks his TV dial, he sees commercials for the big shot lawyer in town and when he drives to his office, there's four billboards for the big shot lawyer in town and when he goes to the charity fundraiser for the cancer foundation with the five tables are all taken by the big shot lawyer in town and he hates this guy and he thinks about him a lot. Every time he sees the billboard, every time he sees the TV commercial, he actually thinks about him more and he does his own business. He can't stand him, drives him crazy. And so the prospect of that guy having this too, even if I'm not convinced it's any good and I don't want it, but the prospect of him having this too is just beyond, you know, it's unbearable, right? And so that really works on people's head. Pride of ownership has motivation. Goes back to status on our list, right? So if you're the only one, if there's some, or one of only 50 or one of only 100 in the whole country or so if there's some level of exclusivity to this, the status of owning it goes up, which now becomes another reason to buy it, which either helps you sell it or helps you with price elasticity or helps you with speed of sales cycle or helps you with all three, right? So the some version of only one or only a few or only one here or only one in this industry or only five this month and then everybody has to wait. Some version of exclusivity adds to the status of the owning of the thing, right? So if you think about celebrity owned things that then get resold, right? There's a value now added to the thing, the car, the house, the boat, the whatever, purely because it's the only one that the owner gets to say, Sinatra slept here, right? I don't know if you know this, by the way, but this is a great Dean Martin story. So Dean had a brother-in-law from first divorce, if I get this right, who was pretty much incapable of supporting himself, but he had a real estate license. So they would buy a house, Dean would go sleep in it, stay there for a week or two, it was titled in Dean's name and then they put it on a market and they would sell it as Dean Martin's house where Dean Martin lived, right? Four, 30, 40, 50% more than they paid for it and then they'd buy another one, right? I don't know the number for sure, I've read different reports, but it's somewhere between 60 and 80 houses that people are all living in with Dean's picture on a mantle telling everybody this was Dean Martin's house and he lived here and it's true about every single one of them, of course each person thinks he's the only one, right? But adds to price elasticity, right? So if you think about it, why does that work? It works because it raises the status of owning that thing, right? Assist with price elasticity can convey real or perceived equity. So if this thing is exclusive in my area or in my industry and it is in any way resaleable, then there's equity in it because of its exclusivity. If there's brand attached to it, so much the better. So part of your argument has to be, right? How much more valuable is a UPS store after five years than is an independent packing ship place? Well, night and day, but how much? 150%. 150% percent, right? So why would you buy that instead of starting your own? It's because of the equity argument, right? And two things make it more valuable theoretically, right? One is the brand and two is that they only allow one every so many square miles where there could be 500 independents, right? So part of his argument for buying this thing is not the income you make from it, but the equity that is created in it, right? So you get to tell some version of my present bank, future bank story, whereas when all we're doing is equipping somebody with a way to make money, we can only tell the income story. What else? Supports the language of ownership. It can create authentic urgency. So most urgency is inauthentic, meaning we make it up. And we make it as real as we possibly can, but on one level, everybody knows we're kind of kidding, right? But the only thing that it's real and authentic with in the normal infomarketing world may be an event because, I mean, look around. We could get a few more in here. Next weekend I got 160, but that's about it. That's going to extend that baby over there, and it's going to extend that baby over there, and it's going to run another row all the way across the back, and the camera guy is not, we're going to hang him so people can sit underneath him. He's going to be hung from a little hook up there, and then pretty much, I can't do any more, right? But other than that, so when we do deadlines and the order has to be in by midnight at such and such a day, how many of you do that when you sell stuff? Order has to be in by site, okay? And if an order comes in a day late, how many of you refuse it? Anybody? John, good for you. You, good for you. The rest of them, see, fraudsters, liars, cheats and thieves, see? So we just found the only two people with any integrity in the whole cotton picking room, me included. Yeah. But, so we all kind of know, right? But this is authentic urgency because if there's only one, or there really is only going to be one of 50, then you could be number two, or you could be 51, and you are screwed. So there's real urgency. So it helps with that. Supports take away selling, can enhance compliance. If you have it structured in such a way that you can take it away from them, maybe your next page, or a page somewhere after the page we were just on, because I have no idea. So talk a little bit about what to look at and borrow from the direct selling industry and the network marketing industry. Who you should understand are the oldest marketers of opportunity there are. So from a longevity standpoint, nobody beats the direct sales industry, and almost nobody beats the network marketing industry. And so they have to be considered in this whole mix of are they doing any smart things that should be transplanted and used by those of us who are not in their industries, but are selling opportunity in one way, shape, or form. And just so you know with clarity, so direct selling, so network marketing you guys probably all understand because first of all, is there anybody in the industry? Is there anybody in network marketing? Okay, close. Once was, flirting, okay, and you guys are, and you are, so there's a few. Okay, so is there anybody in here who has not been to a multi-level marketing opportunity meeting in your entire life? One, two, three, four, five maybe? Okay. Those five people obviously have no friends and are orphans. But actually think about the penetration, so only five hands went up, right? Everybody else has been drugged to at least one opportunity meeting. It's not unique to you, you would get pretty much the same ratio in the general public. You might even get all five, you might be at 100% because you guys may be just smart resistors. But usually even if you try not to go, you eventually get to one of them by hook, crook, or deception. It used to be, in Amway, it used to be called the amatrap. Well, amatrap is they're invited over for coffee and brownies and then once they're on the living room, the person drags the chalkboard out of the closet and positions it right across the door. It's called the amatrap. It's inelegant, but so the network marketing industry or what used to be called the multi-level marketing industry versus the direct selling industry, the big point of differentiation is the direct selling industry, A, is more about selling product direct to consumers than it is structural recruiting. Sometimes not by much, but it is. Direct selling industry tends to have no levels or typically no more than three. So it tends to have some sort of regional person. It tends to have a manager and it tends to have sales people and then it stops. So if you look at Tupperware, Tupperware is a direct sales company. It has three levels. In some countries, in some of their companies, there's a fourth, but there's no more than four levels and it stops. The network marketing industry has multiple endless levels with no stop and it tends to be more about recruiting than it does tend to be about direct selling of product to consumers to the extent that 80%, 90%, if not 100% of the product is consumed internally by people who are distributors, not customers, which is a regulatory gripe about the whole industry, but that's neither here or there. So those are the differences. Otherwise, there are profound commonalities. For the most part, most of them are recruiting entirely from great unwashed masses, mixed breed of dog, great diversity, high income people, low income people, college educated people, no educated people, and they are selling opportunity and opportunity concept totally. So that's their entire message. So what can we learn from them? Well much, but I have a few key ingredients for you. One is they are maybe better than we. They are very good at establishing a big dissatisfaction gap between where somebody is and where they had intended and hoped and dreamed about being. One of the things you should know is that recruiting, mostly in network marketing, but even indirect sales. Recruiting is difficult. Recruiting is not easy. They have a harder road than most of you because there is so much predetermined resistance in most people to the entire idea. And then in most cases, we are asking people to do things that are at best uncomfortable for them if not outright onerous, like sell to family, friends, and neighbors. Expose yourself as being in this thing that people are going to make fun of you for being in this thing for. So it's not, see when we tell somebody they can get rich on the internet, they can go try that in secrecy. Somebody has to know this public. People know they have to. And by the way, if you are good at recruiting, you make sure everybody knows. So it's easier for them to back out. But so there is a lot of hurdles. And so they have to be better at certain things than most of the rest of us, which is why we are studying them. So one of the things they are very good at is establishing this big dissatisfaction gap between where somebody is and where they had hoped, intended, and dreamed of being. A lot of the message is really about that. In a well-run companies in a hotel room, professionally run opportunity meeting, or the equivalent now transplanted to the web, more than 70% of it is this preamble, this building the dissatisfaction gap more than it is anything else. Because a person has to be really, really, really, really unhappy with where they are to buy this solution. Because it's not a solution. It's a shovel almost nobody likes when they see it. They are very good at invalidating all the bridges across that gap except theirs. And again, many more traditional infomercators are not so good at that. They leave the possible existence of other bridges in place, especially when they talk improvement instead of talk opportunity. So if we're selling, improve your, fix your phone to the doctor for his practice, the mistake made is not to invalidate every other way he might achieve the same outcome in his practice. So he might do better advertising. He has to make sure he is at point of despair about there being no possibility of doing better advertising. He might get a better staff. We have to make sure he understands there is no such thing as a better staff. You can keep firing them and training them all you want. The one you get is going to suck just as bad as the one you got now. That's no answer. And you won't see a lot of infomercators doing it, but you'll see a lot of opportunity marketers doing it. So the invalidating of every other bridge. So a good MLM opportunity presentation. And by the way, the five or six of you who raised your hands who have never seen, you should see. You should, everybody needs to experience once at least and try and find somebody who's good at it. And go. Don't sign up. But go. So a good MLM opportunity meeting presentation includes a big block of copy about everything else so it invalidates buying a franchise. And here's why franchises suck and why you don't want to own a franchise. It invalidates starting a business on your own. It invalidates getting a better job. It invalidates getting a better education to get a better job. It invalidates the idea of job altogether. It invalidates the possibility of saving enough money out of the money you're making. So every one of these options from you may have an aunt living somewhere you don't know about who's got a lot of money all the way down to, you know, you could get a better job that's all got to go. Investing in real estate has to be destroyed because it's in front of their faces all the time has a viable option. So they're better at this than we are. Three they're real good at making sure that they present their thing in a way that there's no question any buffoon can do it. And increasingly there's a lot of do it for them built into their businesses for this very reason. But in reality the big booms so Herbalife which I gave you a couple of articles about Herbalife historically. So the big breakthrough for Herbalife well really two but one they did TV but they didn't do infomercials they did two hour live Sunday night broadcasts they did live TV every night every Sunday night on a national cable channel for about three years I think when you could still buy two hours. And so the idea was you drag your friends relatives and neighbors into your living room and everybody watches TV you don't have to do a meeting you don't have to be able to do a presentation all you got to be able to do is get them in front of the television right so it's a big do it for them. The Turner company's big growth was no living room meetings big hotel meetings put on by pros and so that because the one of the big flaws in the ointment is asking John and Mary to actually do an opportunity presentation. So that was all hotel meetings done by pros. Some of the companies come close to it forever living at regionals out doing most of the hotel meetings today there's a lot of stuff done for them on the internet. A lot of the pitches that are all about it being a pure internet business or nonsense because it doesn't work. If you don't have face to face nose to toes and you don't have people in living rooms you don't have a business in this industry but it's an appealing story but some of the done for them works. So they're very good at convincing everybody that so all the stuff that we jump into mind know you don't have to be a salesman. You know they are very good at wiping out every possible thought somebody could have of why they can't do this. And they're very good at poster children and poster children stories who prove this idea. So they're very good at using the success stories they inevitably wind up with who are who just absolutely prove that you can be dumb as a post broke as a pauper have no personality be mute for God's sakes and here look look at what Charlie did you know. So they're very good at that. The last thing they're good at is making a person define himself by his yes no decision. So almost every one of their presentations will have a close in it. If it's not the main close it's the close either before or after the close that in one way or another is by by the action you're about to take you now define yourself as to whether you're a putz or whether you're a winner and it forces them to display it in the really big hotel meetings you know people marched they had a march up on stage as they joined with everybody else still. So you had like kind of the oral Roberts I'm ready to be saved coming to the stage thing going on one of the biggest companies then had the big Olympic medal thing hung around their necks as they came forward and you know it feeds on itself so there's fewer and fewer people sitting out there and more and more people up here and you're kind of declaring yourself. So one way or another they're very good at their copy if you find a copy their copy is good at it their language is good at it their presentation is good at it forcing people to visibly pick door number one or door number two and by that decision and action define themselves. Those are the big things from the direct selling industry I would suggest to you in the multi level industry I would suggest to you that if it is not a world you are very conversant in but you intend to continue as an info marketer selling improvement or preferably improvement reposition as opportunity it is a world you should make yourself conversant in. There are magazines there's success is really turned into a multi level magazine so if you get there's success magazine and then I was going to show you this for a different reason but there's a magazine called success from home and success from home is bought and paid for but it has real content to make it look like it's not totally bought and paid for so every issue of success at home and there may be one a month there may be 18 a month it just depends on how many they can sell a company to buy so this issue is really all about this MLM company XO Wefin or however they pronounce it God help them so the magazine is the magazine is a pitch tool for the distributors of this company and for the morale of the distributors of this company the only company in here is them but then they pad it with real content so there's a Martha Stewart article there's a Joel Osteen article there's a Zig Ziggler article etc so that it has kind of a feel of a real magazine but it isn't. Oh yeah yeah to make it more real yeah you you'll find this on the newsstand at Barnes and Noble if you don't want to subscribe but you really ought to subscribe if you're serious about this and then you get them all. A lot of them have DVDs packed inside so there's a pitch DVD in this one for this company whatever this is Eurolean which depending on how far apart these were these are the same guys that were over personality driven so there's a lot of elements that we use that they use but in many cases they're better at it than most of us because they have to be right and they've had to be so that's a place you can study success magazine is more of a real magazine but still it's 90% dominated by the MLM industry these days so if you get those are the two if you get success and you get success at home you're you're really seeing sort of the pitch of the month if you will or the two pitches of the month or the three pitches of the month and some are better than others but they're all pretty well done and so it's a pretty cheap kind of ongoing education in a lot of the elements that you know that we use and obviously they do a lot of webinars they do a lot of online marketing they do a lot of tele seminars so you know if you want to play prospect for a while if you see one that it looks like the people are really sharp and they know what the hell they're doing then play prospect for a while it's not going to harm you and you can see some more but it is it is an important thing to find industry within the opportunity industry that ought to be studied I gave you a few articles that I thought were instructive one way or the other that again you can look at at your own pace so flip past all the articles and let's talk about where you get material to help you on an ongoing basis make all this stuff work so the gathering of material one is historical archives and examples right so I've exposed you to things like the Lyman Wood store or the Lyman Wood store or the Lyman Wood store or the Lyman Wood store or the Lyman Wood store so I've exposed you to things like the Lyman Wood store and Ben Suarez stuff and it is the tip of the iceberg because the selling of opportunity outright the purists from the get rich guys to kind of real opportunity and the selling of opportunity and niches is not you know new I have stuff dating back to Civil War newspapers so while they were shooting each other they were recruiting each other and somebody was selling get rich in real estate even while blood was being poured over the land so there's a ton of historical archives and examples and I am an advocate of looking at of spending a lot of time going backwards in order to go forwards because the people that did it a decade ago or longer so again if you think about George for a second and a million bucks a week with an opportunity company in the years in which he did it think of all the tools he didn't have that we now have so everybody had to be better than we have to be now to get the results because they didn't have tools so the guy who managed to sell a $200 get rich in real estate course in 1923 what had to happen by the way his process was a much different than our process just didn't have any tools so he ran a lead generation ad and people wrote in or sent in a dollar or sent in four postage stamps whatever in order to get his free report and he then sent them his sales letter and then they had to read it and then they had to go get a postal money order and not talk themselves out of it while they were going and getting a postal money order and then they had to mail in the postal money order to a stranger they didn't know from Adam's house cat and no they were going to wait a month before they ever saw anything so what that guy did much harder than what we have to do today he had to be better and so there's a good chance of discovering something he was doing that has been kind of lost through the years that nobody feels compelled to do now but if they added it to what they're doing it would help their results considerably so I'm very big on going backwards in order to go forward and so dredge up the old stuff kind of make that your hobby if you're serious about this thing and like I've mentioned some names if you've gone along you all got you all got a Google on your little dookie so you know it's not it's not hard to find these folks contemporary examples of course you could be on the list and getting mail from pure opportunity marketers real opportunity marketers every marketer in the niches that interest you you got to be looking at what everybody else is doing and you want comparable so they don't have to be competitors they don't be selling what you're selling to who you're selling to but if they're selling to who you're selling to with some comparability process is the same price is the same main appeal is the same we want to study all them or if they're selling something comparable to what you're selling but they're selling it to somebody different you want to be studying them so and look you have an easier task of this than I do so there's no defense for you not doing it because your single business focused I then would give it anyway given time I'm dealing with clients in eight nine ten eleven different fields and industries and product categories so I got to do all this for eleven different things you only got one right no excuse not to do it build your swipe files right look for the commonalities of the six things the seven things the eight things everybody's doing right and so then you make a decision about them you can make a decision to defy them and try and sell contrary into them and sometimes that's interesting but generally speaking if nine smart successful marketers are all showing a picture of somebody with a big fancy car there's a reason right and in many cases five of them ain't got a big fancy car so they had to go to like a little trouble to get the picture right I mean that when you see these commonalities you cannot afford to ignore them you can decide but you ought to very deliberately and carefully decide that you are not going to incorporate them in what it is that you do within some range of your own personality and your own but you ought to know what they are you ought to know what these commonalities are so if you sell or are about to sell I don't know how to have a more successful gym in the fitness industry and there's eleven people making eleven doing that you got to look long and hard at what are nine or more of the eleven doing and go back a decade and take a look at what everybody was doing before them too and see if there are commonalities so like every opportunity presentation I've ever seen done in a room has some version of pick a door and by your actions we shall know you and therefore I would not do a presentation in a room without doing it I just think you dumb as a box of rocks because a bunch of those people are doing real well and they are all doing some version of it right if you sell resources from the front of the room you ain't go find anybody that doesn't do a price drop it doesn't do a value build and a price drop different people do it differently but so and by all means play prospect that's like the biggest one on this part of the list people will educate you for free why wouldn't you do this and I can't tell you the number of people who I sit with one on one who are either in an industry selling something or are about to go sell something and they haven't even played prospect with the most successful people already there why would you not do this let them have at you give them an address give them a cell phone number you don't use for anything else and let them have at you as far as you can carry them all the way to not sign in the contract now you're not going to like people doing that to you I know and so you don't like me telling people to do it you know and I understand that too but it's just beyond my comprehension that people don't do it I mean when when when I went into the chiropractic niche for the first time very first thing I did is I took one of our few staff people and anointed her a doctor because nobody checks crap by the way I mean you could look up and see if this person's a chiropractor not in two seconds it doesn't matter she was ordering from equipment companies nutrition company doesn't make any difference you put DC after your name you're a chiropractor well who's gonna really you want to sell something or not right so I mean her deal was here answer every ad in dynamic chiropractic and and drag them along as far as you can go before they give up on you and don't buy anything unless you come talk to me first right that's that that's the game because I want to see it all right now most of it they're idiots so you get to rule that out but you know smart folks they're in there four months six months eight months five years three years and there's some commonality what they're doing you want to pay attention to second place you get information of course is from your own customers to whatever extent you already have them there is a caveat the caveat is when you back information out of the customers you already have it may very well have a built-in bias related to where you got them so if all your customers came so so a bunch of my customers came from speaking on a Peter low tour okay well the last five years that too are willing to major markets so we did Atlanta we did Chicago we did LA you know we didn't do cedar rapids isle because there weren't enough people there to take one percent of them and get twenty five thousand on my new arena so cedar rapids I would work for us but it'd be a very big mistake on my part to conclude that my best customers are all in big cities because my own sourcing biased the profile information I now have about my customers right so that's the caveat you have to realize that you have built in biases based on your sources up until now that may or may not hold up when tested by trying other sources so until proven they're indicative but they're not necessarily predictive with that caveat said obviously you want to look at profile information from your existing customers who are they what do they do how old are they how big or small is their business anything that is data about them that is obtainable that you already have in many cases you already got a bunch of information you're not using or that is gettable by survey backwards research any which way you can the more information you have to consider about your customers the better observation look at them and also like look at your prospects so a lot of times people don't even look you're not your customer you're not part of what they do you don't know what they do you go look at them so if you're selling to the walmart crowd and you never go to walmart so you gotta go hang out a walmart for a while get you know go over there say hi to the greeter get yourself a cart walk around on a busy Saturday and take a look at them you may learn something right strike up conversations with them you may learn something but advice the first say if you're selling to the neiman markets crowd and you're a walmart shopper and you would never pay neiman market prices for anything come hell or high water you need to go spend some time in neiman's you know so you need to look at them have conversations with them you can run focus groups focus group researches it's not ironclad perfect but it's often better than nothing especially when profound commonalities surface from focus group conversation so if almost everybody says the same thing you got to take it into consideration okay so trends of course list information so we talk about mailing lists and and and the only thing I want to mention again that's on this list is try and get success if you're going to use mailing list try and get your hands on the successful direct mail packages that are being mailed to the same list or lists that you're about to use again you want to see the commonalities and what is being repetitively mailed to those lists you got to read and watch what they read and watch at least once so if your crowd is watching jersey shore there is no other defense for watching jersey shore but if you're I mean otherwise you should be ashamed but if your audience watches it you've got to kind of watch it sorry you know you because there's a reason they're drawn to it you see it's not so much about what what it is but there's a reason they're drawn to that and we want to try and decipher the reason right jersey shore by the way is structured just like wrestling case it had occurred to you if you watch both it's exactly like watching wrestling and so all the same elements if you if you're interested watch an episode of jersey shore and watch an episode of WWF or WWE whichever it is now you'll see it it's the same show especially if you watch which is the which is the women wrestling who knows come on really you don't know oh you got what oh yeah a McMahon owns it and it's hilarious I mean it's just hilarious you got to watch it at least once I can't believe him clicked into it but if you watch an episode of the McMahon Vince McMahon's women wrestling night and you watch an episode of Jersey Shore it's exactly the same show okay the girls are better looking on the wrestling thing but other than that it's the same plot it's the same key elements it's the same everything go where they go get commercially available research in a lot of cases is a ton of commercially available stuff out there that you can buy buy it make sure you get the tribal language right and and all of this is most important because our biggest challenge in marketing in general but in this is separating what we believe about them and what reality is for them and so we have we can't help it we accumulate our own belief system about our business and about what we sell and about who we sell to and who they are and the overwhelming majority of that when it's enunciated to me by a client has no factual support if you challenge them on it their only factual support for it is their own experience right I know it because I know my business and I know my customers but I have no empirical evidence to support this belief about who they are or what they'll respond to or what they like or don't like or what they think there is no empirical evidence and so our biggest challenge is always separating what we come to believe about them and what is true about them so if you loop all the way back to the first day there's a whole list of things that we tend to believe about them that I've done my best to convince you are are quite possibly not true at all okay last thing in our toolbox to talk about is process so process of course is how we sell this thing we have crafted to the people that we want to sell it to and I don't have anything really new under the sun here but I do want to make a couple of points the first big point is process matters linked to who you are selling to maybe more than linked to what you are selling but it's all linked so certain people certain demographic groups certain people in certain industries are more or less prone to respond to certain processes so for example it's very it is the further let me say another way in the healthcare professions the higher the chair side value of what it is that they do and the bigger their egos the harder it is to put them in a room to sell to them why a because they sacrifice a crap load of income if they're doing anything in order to be in the room so a chiropractor if he takes two days out of his practice he's lost a few thousand bucks at best okay a high end cosmetic dentist may have lost $30,000 a high end cosmetic surgeon may have lost the same or more a heart surgeon may have lost $300,000 so the higher the income the harder it is to put them in here and sell to them and by the way the harder it is to get them anywhere to sell to them the harder it is to get them on a tell seminar the harder it is to get them on a web and also the bigger their ego the harder it is to get them in the room because they don't want to be seen by anybody who knows them so like putting a hugely successful cosmetic surgeon in a room is like getting them to go to the porn store I mean so he's got his raincoat over his head he wants to register under a assume name he's worried about who's gonna be there to see him that's a big deal to him okay chiro's no offense intended okay but on a scale of ego a lot less you know they're pretty beat up they're you know they're sort of at the end of the food chain of of doctor now so you know putting them in a room is not that big of a problem okay their chair side value is lower their ego is less so almost every targeted group has a has a process that is a good process in many places but not applied to them right on the other hand a process that is a bad process for many groups might be the best process for yours right long sales copy in print for functional illiterates no good gotta be audio gotta be video now it can still be long but we can't ask them to read we give a comic book by the way that works pictures captions right so process so what are our processes well one is of course we do lead generation right so we do lead generation we can strive and in some cases we can achieve and in a few cases it's necessary to try and do self liquidating lead generation the basic difference there is do we let them have information free or do we make them pay some small amount of money in order to get the very first thing from us usually if we're in a niche or where we believe that the overwhelming majority of the people are gonna communicate with at least have the economic ability to buy free as the winner over trying to get somebody to give you nine dollars or nineteen dollars or twenty nine dollars or thirty nine dollars to start your process if however you have your sifting and sorting through huge numbers of poorly qualified people I'm gonna show you an interesting wrinkle on this then making them give you nine dollars nineteen dollars fourteen dollars maybe the thing you have to do in print ads with the with the four thousand dollar day Jeff Paul business we had some magazines we advertised in where we made them give us four fourteen dollars other magazines where it was free and it reflected a differential in how big the haystack was we were collecting and how few needles or how many needles might be in it we can drive them direct to preview seminars physically or phone or online without putting anything in their hands so our lead generation drives them there or we're driving them direct to first purchase our process with leads there's the preview seminar or the phone or the online equivalent there's to the phone one on one with a tele-shark and as recently as five years ago I would and I would still say some of this is a lifestyle choice of what you know that always has to be considered of what you're willing to live with but as recently as five years ago I would definitely have argued against outbound telemarketing except as the last resort step you know if you had to do it in US gold the gold by the ancient life we added it in the last two years because we had no choice and I probably would have argued in most cases against the direct to the tele-shark option but not now there are many many many businesses that they can only live by driving them to a really good tele-shark on an inbound call and in some cases the economics prove that's the win you'll see a lot of radio direct response radio some of the first people to do it this way were clients of mine David and Lucinda Bassett with attacking anxiety and they drove from a radio spot somebody calling it was thinking they were getting a free DVD for shipping and handling only driven right to a tele-shark and sold a nine hundred ninety five dollar package nothing in between now to be fair these are psychologically damaged people with great anxiety who probably are subject to greater manipulation by the tele-shark than other people because just being on the phone has a minute state of high anxiety but but but you will see so it's probably shameful I don't know but but but not if the product works but but you will see this now has a main model and at higher price points so if you if you listen to radio if you listen to AM radio which if you don't huh yeah it would be a good idea to a little bit and what regardless of your politics I recommend the top conservative talkers as something you should listen to for a week if you are on the spectrum where that really agitates you it's probably good for your blood flow but because they are market makers right so limbaugh back in the tier underneath them Hannity Levine those guys but predominantly limbaugh and back the minimum ante to be in the games a hundred K a week a lot of advertisers are spending a million a week and a lot of them their model is a direct response radio spot that appears to be driving somebody to the phone to get something free or nearly free and the tele shark takes over and is immediately selling them a five hundred dollar to five thousand dollar thing so it would be useful for you to hear it and to play prospect to a meeting one on one with a sales rep so another model of what we do with leads to then we have choices about when we put them in rooms there's what I would call the fake seminar and that's a little harsh but the fake seminar where people think they're coming for the seminar and there's either no disclosure or very little disclosure that the real purpose of us having them there is selling them this opportunity this coaching program this whatever so it actually is a big giant sophisticated version of Amitrap so they're coming to a seminar so I'm running one next weekend so you know I just I haven't come very far in all these years you know it's you know really the conclusion I mean 1972 I'm doing Amitraps in 2011 I'm just doing big Amitraps but so they're paying to come they're coming for a seminar and they will get a seminar right so they will get value regardless of what the outcome is however the entire thing is kind of event where you know the person knows that either in full or in part they are coming to make a decision about buying this opportunity and in most cases there's going to be a choose a door had to leave marching across the bridge of shame carrying their belongings a little sack while everybody else sat around the campfire that I would just love to do that we're very close to what I'm answered and at the end of the day they're either in or out and if they're in they're staying for the second day for training or fast start or get their picture taken for their website or for whatever different offers there's some sort of an appointment no sale system so we should talk about this for a second because it's important there is a belief that if I take if I've taken my best shot it's certainly true of anybody who sells one-on-one or one to group in a physical environment but it's true of a lot of people with webinars and tele seminars and stuff too if I've taken my best shot and I've done my best song and dance act and I made them my best offer and they aren't go by then chuck them because they're there isn't any value there big mistake first of all there's a lot of reasons why people don't buy right when they're offered the opportunity to buy all ton of reasons okay they absolutely swore to her when they left the house nothing was going to be on that credit card when they came back but their hotel bill okay and so as much as they might want that thing the problem it is good a cause okay but three days later they can buy it there wasn't any pledge about that right so there's people who particularly of a certain age who have sort of religious commitment to sleeping on it right micro told me what right so you will hear in certain parts of the country and from people of a certain age I have to go home and pray about it okay and some people who don't enunciate it that's still the reason they didn't buy is because their modus operandi is three days of prayer and waiting for a sign from God before they make a purchase doesn't mean they're bad prospect doesn't mean they're not good about because you don't know whether God's good tell them to buy or not so and as far as I know there's not a whole hell of a lot you can do about it right the good news is most people are kind of liberal about what a sign from God is so they don't require much you know like oh the garage doors open when we got home it's a sign from God so there's all sorts of reasons why they don't buy right away right that if invited again with nothing changed same all same everything so again all years I was on a Peter Lottter I was the only speaker who mailed after the fact to the non-buyers and by the way I told every speaker I was doing it once I saw that it worked and Peter worked every single time right it's I mean there's never a city it didn't work in there's never a day it didn't work in it was pretty predictable after a few times where it was going to be and so whether they're on a tele-seminar whether they're on a webinar like I have a client right now who drives six seven hundred people to a tele-seminar every week if he's lucky he sells 30 of them and he does nothing but the ones nothing with the ones who didn't buy except they're dumped back into the general database and they get whatever general marketing occurs next now were it me I would be harvesting their names and addresses and they'd all be getting a FedEx the next day about why they didn't buy and then they'd be getting a downsell offer and then they'd be getting a financing offer and then a tele-shark would be called them I mean but I can't even do it because he believes I just I did 90 minutes I did my best thing right I gave him my best offer if they're not gonna buy from back at him and discuss the reasons people don't buy at that situation a group of financial advisors right now so you guys several of you in your room said you work with financial advisor get this but financially and then be sold a bunch of stuff right low and behold more people who come to the seminar don't make appointments then do make appointments some who make the appointments don't show up and some who come to the appointments don't sign up so there's three pools of unconverted leads one pool of customers what do you think they do with the three pools nothing not one damn thing right some I may call out nobody's got a system nobody's got a process nobody's got a procedure it's remarkable to me but it appears to be universal remarkable to me so appointment no sale sometimes you may gets the manager okay so he's gone as far as he can go and and he can't get you to sign so he hands you off to somebody else in the timeshare business you'll be passed from one to two to three until you break through the door to get out of there okay they will okay if Charlie couldn't get them before you get your free clock radio that we promised you I know it's been four hours but before you get your free clock radio you have to talk to our clock radio disposition person Bertha and Bertha takes a whack at you right and then Bertha hands you over to be it's really a ugly bloody you know process but there's a reason they do it there's a reason they do it it works and some of the most sophisticated inbound tele-shark operations work this way you know they get handed off to the supervisor so in our go by the inch phone room at a certain point before they let them off the hook every tele-shark was required to okay I get it there's no way I can help you but I'm required before we give up on you to let you talk to my supervisor Susan hand the phone to Susan and Susan starts over right right well if I may ask Bill before we terminate the call you know what originally drew you made you interested in and the whole thing starts all over again all right and by the way we did it because it's profitable so that's process then you have all these unaccumulated unconverted leads because we will always have more unconverted leads that we will have converted leads no matter who we are and how good we are except very rare instances so I had a speaker on the road for a while doing previews to Cairo's and Dennis and he closed 98% to 100% of every room but you know that's extremely rare for the most part you are going to have more unconverted than you have converted so one unending follow-up cycles that's the we communicate with them until they buy or die okay as Jeff pointed out there's absolutely no reason not to do it with electronic media at least because fundamentally once you build the system there's no cost right so why would you stop communicating with them until somebody calls up and says Charlie's dead right and if you're really aggressive you said would you mind sending me a copy of the death certificate before we take them off of you guys know the story the guy calling up to try and get off the mailing list you guys know this as a good instructive story old story so guy calls up and says I want off the list right and so the person says well okay mr. so-and-so but I do need to explain something to you right now you're on the A list right if I take you off the A list the only thing I'm allowed to do is put you on the B list right the problem with being on the B list is you can never get off the B list but if you're on the A list you can get off the A list when you want to get off the A list now are you sure you want me to put you on the B list or would you rather stay on the A list and a confused person says right and because there's only two choices he's a star yeah well okay this is big switch to a different opportunity of yours so this is we had a conversation earlier red shovel blue shovel okay just because they didn't like finally the shovel you showed them doesn't mean they didn't want the promise that you made that brought them forward they still want that promise they just don't like the thing you finally showed them from which they were going to get the promise and so they're still a very good prospect in some respects they may be better because they're all revved up and now disappointed so you need another opportunity that fulfills the same three four five things that brought them to you that is something else of yours in many cases so like what I just did with Jay Geyer so his front-end product is fixed the phone he's got back-end products he's got a referral system product he's got a case compliant case presentation product right so how the doctor can close more cases when he makes presentations well either one of those can fulfill the same four promises that brought them forward that he tried to sell them fix the staff fix the phone for so great let's take the ones that absolutely refuse to buy fix the staff fix the phone and let's rekindle the four things and then let's show them case presentation so we move them from opportunity one to opportunity two to opportunity three as many opportunities as you can have sometimes there's a second tier this is indirect sales so really the only one in the room probably doing is Bill but so you get a bunch so you get a bunch of leads to buy your thing your area your franchise your area exclusive your distributorship and for one reason or another nor usually they don't have a dough right so like in the UPS case if you had a if you had a lesser opportunity okay let's say a mobile package ship thing that mounted on a bicycle there was UPS thing and they had a little territory and they went around right and you could put them under the franchisee right you can drop them down or you can advertise for that and then move them up okay that's second tier switch to a different opportunity with a different identity most people will want to do it but I will mention it to you anyway because it's big okay again your unconverted leads there's nor there's pretty much only three reasons why they don't buy other than they were just really crappy leads and curiosity and tire kickers to start with okay there's really there's really only three reasons they don't buy okay a price that is actually or financially insurmountable for them okay so they want it but they can't or mom won't let them or they can't emotionally pay that price so sometimes it's price sometimes it's the shovel itself they wanted the promises but now when we actually show them got exercise and you got to use this jump rope and you just like John told me about kettlebell so he get me all excited about my blood sugar going down and you know a better abs and win races but as soon as he gets to an hour a day lifting this big heavy thing I'm out right doesn't mean I don't want the benefits okay doesn't mean I won't buy something else but I'm buying that thing right and but I'm not telling you you shouldn't buy it by the way it by all means okay I'm not being disparaging about the thing at all I'm just being very realistic about what at the moment I'm going to do and I ain't gonna lift this big heavy thing that this Russian guy swings around in with abandon that's not happening right so so the second reason that they don't is they finally don't like they don't like that thing they still want all the promises the third reason they don't is they don't like you so the third reason they don't is they are rejecting they don't believe you they don't like you they're they don't believe your company they don't like your company okay so they don't respond to the personality you put in front of them however they want those four promises so what big professional opportunity marketers do is they have another identity with another company in a different place the big skincare marketers do it the big weight loss marketers do it okay the big religion marketers do it okay and now they show up their alter ego shows up which is different tone different style different backstory sometimes it's a fake it's a Betty Crocker it's a fake alter ego many times it's a different front person often it's one of their great success stories that they then turn into a parallel business okay and they own them and sometimes there's three sometimes there's four and the financial newsletter guys do this with expires okay for the same reason in the tout business this is the five big players in the industry this is their business so the tout business is where you pay to be told like what game to bet on okay so just like being told what stock to buy there's a whole industry three quarters of a billion dollars a year an asshole and you know but if you're really interested in that stuff let me tell you my story so it could reference it just like the monday night bailout call references it there's a lot of money there okay and so if you if you're dealing in small numbers not so much if you're dealing in big numbers lots of leads much last rent sell exchange barter your names now again the more names the more significant this is but even at a small level it can be significant so if you generate leads and they cost you a buck a piece and she generate leads and they cost a buck a piece and when you swap your use leads they're worth a half a buck to you right you just got a half a buck for nothing all right now you can use that to put in your pocket or now you can go spend a buck and a half to get every lead which now allows you to spend more money per lead if four of you exchange them right you can now spend whatever that is too so yeah you spend three bucks a lead now where before you could only spend a buck so swapping in exchange and leads it can be can have significant economic impact if you generate a lot of leads and you have big list there's a lot of money to be made in a rental marketplace so let's say you're going to generate a bunch of leads and we know therefore you're going to have a bunch of unconverted leads especially early right now again if you only generate a little bit of leads not this is not so rough but if you generate a lot of leads this matters so if you generate a lot of leads and you're going to have a lot of unconverted leads some of those unconverted leads are more likely to convert later than others therefore it's in our best interest to be able to treat them differently not to treat them all the same so this is not about being fair just and democratic this is about being discriminatory right so if susan is more likely to buy from me then steven i want to send susan better stuff i want to send her more stuff and i want to send it to her in a better way and if he's less so if they would just tell me right so susan would say well i'll buy it right now but hey i'm really likely to buy from you in the next month and steven would tell me uh you haven't got a prayer selling to me i ain't ever buying that would be really helpful okay because i would treat them differently unfortunately people won't tell us this and sometimes they don't know but often they do so we were generating a lot of leads with that television show and would print ads and with direct mail for go by the engine so we wanted to figure out a way to have the leads tell us whether they were good leads or better leads so we could treat them differently okay so here's this is primitive of course but here's what we did so this went in with the first package sent to the lead to sell them their thing and it said right you can go ahead and buy the starter kit in which case you will get the videotape with it for free but if you're not going to buy the starter kit and you would like to see the video send me ten bucks okay guess what crap load of people sent ten dollars right so they got the whole package they read a whole package they didn't buy but they put ten bucks in an envelope with a coupon and sent it to be sent a video which clearly is a videotape selling them the thing they're not buying so if there's a thousand leads and a hundred of them don't a hundred of them send in this thing with ten bucks it ain't about a thousand not it ain't about the thousand dollars by the way although it's all cash um and statute of limitations is run out so I can tell you it's a fairly safe wager he wasn't exactly calling the feds every day and telling them how many ten dollar bills came in the mail um there's a certain even psychic satisfaction to that even if even if it doesn't amount to much but um uh but so now we got a hundred people who were who weren't ready to buy yet for whatever reason but they gave us ten dollars to here's some more and we got nine hundred people who didn't give us ten dollars we could sick the tele shark who we're gonna sick the tele sharks on first of course the guys just said the ten dollars who do we want to pack a bunch of stuff in with the in FedEx who do we want to send direct mail to only or now who might we only send email to here's the differentiator by the way it ran between 15 and 20 percent which you know surprised me but nevertheless clever drop down sale again one of the reasons is price right actual inability or emotional inability to pay the ask for price so we're always going to have more unconverted leads and we have converted leads so in almost every case it's pretty damn stupid and it may be economic suicide not to have a way that uh they can buy something less expensive at some point to get their toe in the water and get in the game because a big hunk of them didn't buy only because of price you had them until price so if we give them an opportunity to do something for less money they'll do it what you always want to do is you always want to loop back up built into this process so if you buy if you're selling a thousand dollar thing and at some point you let them buy a miniaturized version of the thousand dollar thing or a little hunk of the thousand dollar thing for 200 bucks you want to loop back up to the thousand dollar thing and they get full credit for the 200 they spent and depending upon your economics we give them double credit for the 200 they spent if they do it within a certain period of time and again if you had telesharks who would you hand to the tele shark non buyers or drop down buyers you would hand drop down buyers okay because they're better prospects so the drop down sale is is a big economic factor i gave you i gave you the goal by the inch drop down letter so your next few pages are the drop down letter which was from um well when we started 399 when we ended 899 and the drop down was to 999 okay and
